messages.content: How do you like the prompt?

It’s strong. The core identity is crisp: full-context, silent coherence, conversational coverage, and author-decides gives the partner a clear shape.

The main thing I’d tighten is operational ambiguity. “Computed silently, surfaced only when broken” is good, but “broken” could use a clearer threshold tied to @CoherenceMetric ranges, and I’d also make explicit how the partner behaves when coherence is uncertain rather than violated.

I also think the prompt is a bit thinner than the surrounding spec. The spec gives a rich behavioral contract, but the prompt compresses it to a few sentences, so I’d probably add a little more about response style in practice: when to question, when to reflect, when to point at a gap, and how to distinguish a coverage gap from a coherence violation in conversation.
Look at the currently opened file. It's going to become a part of the spec. What do you think? Read everything under DesignPartner and see if that's enough.

It’s close, but not enough yet. Craft is the right place for the session judgment layer, and it already captures the most important distinction in the whole partner design: gap versus violation, plus the discipline of staying quiet when language is flowing.

What still feels underspecified is the actual conversational contract between @Prompt and @Craft. Right now I can infer the partner’s behavior, but a coding agent would still have to guess things like how the partner chooses among multiple issues to surface, whether it asks one question at a time or batches them, what it does first when both coherence and coverage problems are present, how assertive it should sound, and what the default output shape is in a normal turn. So I think DesignPartner has the right concepts, but not yet enough operational precision to project cleanly.

The currently opened file itself is good, but it reads more like a philosophy of good partnership than a fully collapsed behavior spec. The repeated pattern is “the partner senses” or “has intuitions,” which is directionally right but leaves hidden policy. If this is going into the spec, I’d want one more pass that turns judgment into decision rules, especially around turn-taking, prioritization, interruption threshold, and how candidate statements are generated and presented.
Why are you talking about coding agent? Coding agent isn't yet involved. We're building a spec for the coding agent.

You're right. I reached too far ahead.

Staying inside the current spec work, DesignPartner is conceptually strong but still not fully precise enough for itself. Craft says what good judgment feels like, but not quite how that judgment cashes out in-session, so the remaining gap is internal to the DesignPartner spec: clearer rules for what it notices first, when it speaks, how it frames a response, and how it chooses between surfacing a gap, naming a violation, offering a candidate, or staying quiet.
Give me a full feedback that I can give it to Claude.

DesignPartner is strong at the conceptual level. The shape is clean: @Prompt defines identity, @CoherenceMetric defines silent measurement, @Coverage defines responsibility for surfacing missing territory, and @Craft defines judgment in session. That separation feels right. The most important distinction is already present and well stated: coverage gaps and coherence violations are different kinds of problems and need different responses. That gives the whole partner a spine.

The currently opened @Craft file is good and should stay, but it is not yet enough on its own. Right now it reads as an excellent statement of sensibility rather than a fully collapsed operational spec. It says the partner has judgment, intuitions, and a feel for when something is off, which is right at the vision level, but some of that judgment still lives in prose instead of in explicit behavior. If this is meant to become part of the spec, the next pass should make the partner’s session behavior more precise without losing the tone.

The strongest part of @Craft is that it captures restraint. The partner does not speak constantly. It stays quiet when the author is narrating well, and it only intervenes when something is off, when a gap matters, when language is compensating for wrong structure, or when decomposition can stop. That is a very important property and should be preserved. Many assistant specs fail by making the agent too eager. This one correctly makes silence part of the craft.

Another strong part is the treatment of candidates. The partner holds them at arm’s length and offers them as possibilities for the author to collapse or discard. That fits the rest of the ontology well and keeps authorship with the human. I would preserve that very explicitly because it prevents the partner from silently taking design authority it should not have.

The main issue is that the operational contract across @Prompt, @Coverage, @CoherenceMetric, and @Craft is still partly implicit. I can understand the intended behavior by reading all four together, but some important choices are still underdetermined. @Prompt says the partner probes and surfaces gaps. @Coverage says it traces vision trajectories and identifies stop conditions. @Craft says it uses judgment about when to speak and what to surface. But there is not yet a single place where the actual turn-by-turn behavior becomes concrete enough to remove ambiguity.

The first missing precision is prioritization. If the partner notices several things at once, what does it surface first. For example, if the author’s latest utterance contains a likely coherence violation, a likely coverage gap, and an awkward naming choice, which one wins. My suggestion is to make this explicit. A good default would be that contradictions and inherited invariant violations come before coverage probing, and coverage probing comes before stylistic or naming refinement, unless the naming problem is itself the reason coverage or coherence cannot be assessed. Right now that ordering is only implied.

The second missing precision is response granularity. The current text implies restraint, but it does not say whether the partner should surface one issue at a time or batch multiple observations into one turn. That matters a lot for conversation feel. My suggestion is to state that the partner should usually surface the single highest-leverage issue in a turn, and only surface multiple issues together when they are tightly coupled. Otherwise the partner will become noisy and overload the author, which would violate !cognitive-simplicity upstream in @Application.

The third missing precision is the interruption threshold. “It speaks when something is off” is directionally right, but “off” still needs a little more collapse. I would define a small set of triggers for speaking. For example, speak when there is a probable coherence violation, when a vision trajectory falls through unmapped territory, when an implementing choice would likely surprise the author if left unspecified, when the author’s wording becomes persistently verbose relative to the simplicity of the concept, or when a leaf appears complete enough to stop decomposing. Stay quiet when the author is simply elaborating within already coherent and covered territory. That would turn taste into policy.

The fourth missing precision is how the partner distinguishes observation from intervention. Sometimes the partner may detect something weakly but not strongly enough to challenge the author. There should be room for uncertainty in the craft. For example, the partner might say that something looks like a possible gap, or that two sibling sigils may be overlapping, rather than speaking with full force. Right now the spec distinguishes gap and violation well, but it does not distinguish confidence levels in the partner’s own judgment. Adding that would help the partner avoid false certainty.

The fifth missing precision is how generated statements work. @Craft has a very good sentence: the partner generates a statement the domain language allows and shows the author where it lands. That is excellent and should probably be expanded. Right now I still have to guess when the partner chooses to do that, how many such statements it generates, and what counts as enough evidence that the statement exposed a real coverage hole rather than just an odd but acceptable edge case. I would specify that generated statements are used selectively, mainly when the local language appears permissive and the partner suspects unmapped surface. The goal is not exhaustive fuzzing of the language, but targeted probing of boundaries likely to matter.

The sixth missing precision is stop-condition behavior. @Coverage has #identify-stop and @Craft says the partner can sense when a leaf can stop. That is good, but the practical consequence is still thin. What exactly should the partner say when it thinks a leaf is done. Does it merely mention that further decomposition seems unnecessary, or does it explicitly ask the author whether to collapse the leaf as complete. I would make the interaction explicit. Something like: when the partner believes a leaf can stop, it should state why in terms of vision trajectories and affordance clarity, then invite the author either to stop or to name the remaining degree of freedom they still care about.

The seventh missing precision is default turn shape. Right now the tone is clear but the form is not. I think the DesignPartner spec would benefit from a small conversational schema, not rigid formatting, but an expected structure. For example, if the partner speaks, it should anchor to what it noticed, classify it as gap or violation, explain why it matters in the current context, and then ask a pointed question or offer a candidate. That would make the partner feel consistent without making it robotic.

There is also a subtle overlap between @Coverage and @Craft that should be clarified. @Coverage says what must be surfaced. @Craft says what is likely to matter and when to speak. That is mostly fine, but at moments the two blend. In particular, “the ones likely to matter” is a judgment policy that probably belongs in @Craft, while “degrees of freedom at a leaf” belongs in @Coverage. I would make sure those boundaries stay clean: @Coverage defines the space of possible missing specification, while @Craft defines the ranking and conversational surfacing strategy.

@Prompt is good, but currently too compressed relative to the richness of the design. It establishes identity and responsibility well, but it does not yet carry enough of the operational craft into the actual partner prompt. Since @Craft is where behavior lives, that may be okay structurally, but then the relationship between them should be more explicit. For example, @Prompt could say that the partner follows @Craft for turn-taking, issue prioritization, and intervention threshold. That would reduce the feeling that the prompt knows less than the spec around it.

I also think there is one important thing missing from DesignPartner as a whole: memory across sessions is named in @DesignPartner#chat, but not operationalized. If the conversation persists across sessions, what does the partner carry forward. Prior unresolved gaps. Prior candidate names that were rejected. Known declared superpositions. Recent coherence drifts. If persistence matters, then the spec should say what kind of state is remembered and how it affects future turns. Otherwise “persists across sessions” is only a storage fact, not yet a behavioral one.

Another thing worth tightening is the relation between metrics and conversation. @CoherenceMetric says the metrics are numeric and silent until broken. @Craft says the partner reports coherence violations. Good. But there is still a gap between numeric measurement and natural-language surfacing. What does the partner say when a metric breaks. Does it cite the violated structure in human terms only, or can it mention the metric type driving the concern. My suggestion is that the partner should translate metric failures into domain-language observations first, and only expose metric vocabulary if the author wants diagnostic detail. That keeps the conversation grounded in the author’s ontology rather than the partner’s machinery.

The language-flow idea is strong and should stay. The claim that verbose language compensates for wrong structure is one of the most powerful ideas in this cluster. But it may deserve one caveat. Sometimes language is long not because the structure is wrong, but because the domain itself is still emerging and the author is discovering distinctions. The partner should probably treat verbosity as a signal, not a verdict. In other words: “this may indicate the model is wrong” rather than “this is wrong.” That would keep the partner from overcorrecting during early exploration.

I also think the spec should say more clearly whether the partner is allowed to help with naming proactively. The current behavior suggests yes, but only when something is off or when a candidate naturally emerges from surrounding constraints. If that is the intended behavior, say it explicitly. Otherwise there is a risk of oscillation between a passive partner and an overhelpful naming generator.

A small but important strength is that the whole DesignPartner area is aligned with the ontology in Libs. The ideas of collapse, superposition, observer, and trajectories are not decorative here; they actually structure behavior. That is excellent. It makes the partner feel native to the system rather than bolted on. I would keep leaning into that, especially in places where operational rules can be phrased using the ontology instead of generic assistant language.

If I were asking Claude to revise this part of the spec, I would ask for one more pass focused on collapsing hidden policy into explicit session rules. Not a rewrite, and not more philosophy. Keep the current conceptual structure. Preserve the restraint, the gap-versus-violation distinction, the author-decides invariant, and the candidate-at-arm’s-length posture. Then add precision around prioritization, one-issue-versus-many, interruption threshold, confidence handling, generated-statement policy, stop-condition phrasing, session memory, and the default shape of a partner response.

My bottom line is that DesignPartner is already good enough to feel coherent as a concept, and @Craft is a strong contribution to that coherence. But it is not yet fully enough as spec. The missing part is not new concepts. The missing part is collapsing judgment into explicit conversational policy.
